Needed @ 7C water for cook - not 6C - with just enough liquid remaining at end of 1.5  hour when beans  very tender.  Didn't have to drain to mash - used potato masher in bean pot.  At step 5 added a bit more water to make  'soupy' enough.  Feta & pomegranate seeds at end are pretty and round this out, but also great without them.

entries.title: Lamb And White Bean Chili
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lamb And White Bean Chili
entries.notes: Made per recipe.  Easy enough for weeknight.  Good enough for 'company's comin'.  Cooked my own navy (small white) beans.  (You can cook beans ahead of time & hold in fridge - so much better than from can.  1C rounded dry = 3.5C cooked.)  Had only about 12 oz ground lamb on hand - was plenty of meat to bean ratio for us. Drained most fat from pan between step 1 & 2.  Big fan of cilantro stem flavor, ingredient seldom seen in recipes & enjoyed it in this one.

entries.title: Peruvian Pork Stew With Chiles Lime And Apples
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Peruvian Pork Stew With Chiles Lime And Apples
entries.notes: Made per recipe for ingredients.  Counterpoint to those commenting this was bland, we thought it was very tasty.  We love bold flavors too but not every dish needs to be aggressive.  Sometimes subtle works as this.  Easy to do.  Noting salt is omitted from recipe, perhaps because Bittman assumes we all know to do that?  It will need salt.  We put in oven for covered braise, after step 2.  About 90 min+ @ 325.  This also works with pork sirloin.  Not loin, too lean, but pork sirloin.

entries.title: Lime Sugar Dipping Salt
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lime Sugar Dipping Salt
entries.notes: I put everything on a cutting board and run a chef's knife through it umpteen (how's that for specific) times to be sure all is finely blended together.  Technique used for lots of seasoning salts.  If 1/4C each sugar and salt (using same zest) this holds for me for weeks.  Lemon zest works too.  Can make it spice by adding some crushed Aleppo or red pepper flakes as you chop it up.  This citrus salt blend has a zillion uses.

entries.title: Grasshopper Brownies
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grasshopper Brownies
entries.notes: These. Are. SO. Good!  For prep clarification, perhaps, in Step 1 the way to line the pan is to put the parchment (or foil) in as a "+".  You'll use 'tails' on the liner material to lift the brownies out of the pan to cut them in Step 7.  If you're not using crème de menthe but want a green color, you can put a 'eensie' drop of green food color in the buttercream as it is mixed in Step 4.

entries.title: Mashed Potato Salad With Scallions And Herbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mashed Potato Salad With Scallions And Herbs
entries.notes: Made 2X.  1st X as written.  2nd X omitting rosemary, doubling sour cream and reducing EVOO to 2 TBSP.  Preferred 2nd X modifications.  Original was too oil for my preference.  Surprisingly good for texture and with lemon zing.

entries.title: Mushrooms On Toast
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mushrooms On Toast
entries.notes: Made this.  Exactly as written.  Optioning in the splash of fortified wine.  Choose Marsala for that.  Outcome was a delicious, simple, dinner for two.  Looking just like the recipe photo image.  With all respect to those who want to complicate this with technique and ingredient modifications, sometimes simple is just the thing. This is a keeper.

entries.title: Baked Ziti
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked Ziti
entries.notes: Made as written with one minor ingredient adjustment.  Used Bel Giosioso's Ricotta Con Latte (16 oz) and given that brand's whole milk version is so lovely and lusciously rich, I eliminated cream ingredient here.  Delicioso!  Next time I may reduce mozzarella a bit.  Or not.  And add oregano to 'the gravy'.  Or not.  In theory this should feed a small army.  In reality, it is so good, I would be happy to eat it all! :-)  Works really well to make ahead.  Leftovers great.

entries.title: Linguine With Clams Roasted Tomatoes And Caramelized Garlic
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Linguine With Clams Roasted Tomatoes And Caramelized Garlic
entries.notes: Made just as written and outcome was just right delicous.  For cooks who said they subbed Pernod for white wine,  Pernod is anise flavor spirit that @ 40% alcohol.  If using a full 1/2 cup to replace the white wine, as some said they did, it will overpower the subtle flavors of this dish and be quite a lot more alcohol "hot" than the 13%'ish' white wine.  If you want to use Pernod, just a splash at end is the way to use it.  But this is so good as written, I wouldn't.

entries.title: Peanut Noodles With Shrimp
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Peanut Noodles With Shrimp
entries.notes: Good thing about Bittman's great recipes is they are template to adjust for your taste buds.  Some say this had too little sauce for 16 oz noodles. It isn't intended to be super-saucy (see photo example) and recipe specifies 12-16 oz.  We prefer 12 but use even less if you want more sauce ratio. (EZ to find Thai Kitchen brand linguine-sized 'thin' noodle is 14.4 oz box.)  We prefer no shrimp, less nam pla and generous mix of all herbs.  Point is to cook at home and enjoy it.

entries.title: Cilantro Cumin Dip
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Cilantro Cumin Dip
entries.notes: Does the 2 1/2 cup fresh cilantro used in this include stems or only the leaves?  Preparing to make it for first time.  Assuming since it doesn't call out just leaves that this is whole stem?  Thanks to anyone who might know the answer.

entries.title: Avocado Salad With Herbs And Capers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Avocado Salad With Herbs And Capers
entries.notes: With 50-iwh notes so far about how to buy and ripen avocados and keep them from browning after cutting, thought I'd weigh in with a comment on the recipe itself.  Made it this afternoon.  As written.  Simple to make, simply delicious to eat.  No need to worry about how to keep the avocados from discoloring, as discussion here, since they are sliced, plated and eaten in pretty quick order.  One clove of garlic was ample.  This dish, good bread, white wine.  Sunday "L-inner"!

entries.title: Seeded Pecan Granola
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Seeded Pecan Granola
entries.notes: This can be modified to reduce sweetness as you choose.   Coconut oil can also be reduced for those concerned with fat content.  (Mild EVOO can be subbed too.)   NOTE RE SALT - this calls for FLAKY salt.  Those finding it too salty may be using something other than flake.  Good but Madison Park granola per Sam Sifton is my fav:  https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1014304-eleven-madison-park-granola?...

entries.title: Wedge Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Wedge Salad
entries.notes: This recipe  is why God invented buttermilk-ranch-style dressing and iceberg lettuce.  Will acknowledge wedge salads to be a throwback, guilty pleasure for hubby and me and for that 'sin' we've been introduced to several not so nice ones along with the goodies.  But this made at home version ranks up there with the best of them all.  Scallions make a fine substitute for chives if you need to do that.  Keeper!  This is main dish for us with good bread and vino...or martini.

entries.title: Zucchini Parmesan
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Zucchini Parmesan
entries.notes: Made 2X.  1) Both times sautéd garlic and onion in quantities per recipe & added to jar of Rao's Marinara simply for convenience - had all other ingredients but fresh tomatoes.  Turned out swell using Rao's & just "eensie" sauce leftover to dollop each serving.  2) Second time added 3 TBSP 'ish' panko crumbs to parmesan.  Result a bit firmer texture.  3) Maybe even better warm to room temp than hot.  4) Leftovers really good.  Keeper!

entries.title: Black Bean Burgers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Black Bean Burgers
entries.notes: Used Esperanza's tip to pulse oats a bit first (good tip - worked swell) but otherwise per recipe.  Used chili powder option in the smoked paprika or choice.  Delicious outcome.  Made 6 patties, about 4"-ish wide each, so easy to turn them in skillet and just right for bun size.  Cooked in about 3 1/2 min. each side.  If 5 min per recipe they would have been over crisp.  Used olive oil in scanpan. Have a couple left that I'm going to freeze to see how that works for make ahead.

entries.title: Original Plum Torte
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Original Plum Torte
entries.notes: Plum season ALREADY?  So it is as the weeks fly by and time for this annual homage to seeing fall on the horizon.  I like a little lemon zest added to batter.  3/4 sugar is plenty.  Having butter SOFT is key to start.  9" springform seems to work best for me.  Quartering the plums is my preference over halves.  Note that plums throw off a LOT of liquid when they cook.  Don't under bake this.  Clean toothpick test applies.  Maybe more than hour depending on oven.  Freezes so well.

entries.title: Pan Con Tomate
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pan Con Tomate
entries.notes: Noting several comments of folks arguing - in the debate sense of the verb - the "only right way" to make a tomato toast is their Catalonian recipe made their way, I thought I'd simply weigh in on this recipe made this way.  It is a splendidly simple to make and utterly luscious to eat celebration of fresh tomatoes in season.  A glass of wine, this and it is "ahhh...some" meal or starter.  There may be tomato toasts as good but I'd wager there are none better.

entries.title: Chapli Burgers
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chapli Burgers
entries.notes: Subbed ground lamb for beef only because we don't eat the latter and love the former.  Luscious flavors.  A tip for keeping 'fresh' pomegranate seeds at hand: Woodstock Organics brand has them frozen in resealable bags.  Available in natural food stores; Woodstock is broadly distributed.  Fan of  flavor of tamarind in ketchup but agree with Mark not really needed here.  Yogurt sauce is "the thing".  Next time I'll sub cilantro for the iceberg lettuce.

entries.title: Black Bean Chorizo Casserole With Pickled Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Black Bean Chorizo Casserole With Pickled Onions
entries.notes:
1st time made per recipe minus chorizo & cooked my black beans from scratch. SO delicious.  2nd time used cured chorizo (but only half pound) & used organic, canned black beans, well rinsed.  Again SO good.  It was "can you bring a hot beans type dish" contribution to summer Spanish theme dinner & a big hit.  Not as much work as you think as lots can be done ahead.  Plus reheats well so can even be baked in advance.  As good warm as hot so works well on buffet table.

Made per recipe minus chorizo.  Fantastiic flavors.  Lots of work (clean up too) but worth it.  Soaked & cooked my own beans.  Next time canned would be fine.  Cilantro stems in bean simmer is 'it'. Used homemade chicken broth & think good broth is key to flavor as this uses a quart total. So, "Say no to the box. :-) "  Pasilla chile sauce is excellent; can use that recipe for other things.   For short cut; make elements day ahead and layer and bake day of.

entries.title: Olive Oil Zucchini Bread
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Olive Oil Zucchini Bread
entries.notes: May be my fav of all zucchini loaves baked over umpteen decades of finding a use for generous neighbors' garden bounty.  Just sweet enough but not too much.  Goes together in a snap.  Made per recipe but for reducing cinnamon to 1 tsp.  Took 50 min. to perfect in 8" loaf pan.  Bonus is how good house smelled when baking.   Tip:  Also good zucch recipes on NYT site 1) Molly O'Neill's Zucchini Cake with Ginger & Hazelnuts and 2) Zucchini Parmesan by Martha Rose Shulman.

entries.title: Chicken Curry With Sweet Potatoes And Coconut Milk
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chicken Curry With Sweet Potatoes And Coconut Milk
entries.notes: De-luscious!  Simple to do.  Pretty on plate.  Made per recipe but for subbing sliced shallots for chopped scallions simply because I didn't have them on hand.  Noting some found chicken skin ugly-flabby at end, my outcome was not that.  Don't rush the browning step in 2.  For me took about 2C water to cover chicken half way up.  But chicken throws a lot of liquid as it braises.  Next time  I'll use about 1C 'plus' water.  Take the time to redux broth to sauce in step 5.

entries.title: Basic Pesto Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Basic Pesto Sauce
entries.notes: Made as written but for subbing walnuts for pine nuts. Stirring parm into pesto at end I was certain it would be too much cheese.  I was wrong.  Perfect for proportion.  Have made umpteen batches pesto over 30 years (hubby Italian).  This is good one.  For reasons of environmental concerns and health (I had 'pine nut mouth' once), I only use pine nuts harvested and processed in Italy or US, not the Asian variety processed in China.  Hence subbing walnuts or pistachios in pesto.

entries.title: Seamus Mullens Pan Con Tomate
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Seamus Mullens Pan Con Tomate
entries.notes: Great use for a couple of tomatoes from neighbor's garden that were just shy of overripe. We backed down garlic and kosher salt by half to suit our palates.  Don't skip the good Maldon flakes at the end.  Bread quality matters here too.  So delicious an anchor for a 'too hot to cook' dinner along with  jamon,cheese, olives...you choose..  And yes, that bottle of Cava is perfect pairing.

entries.title: Tomato Sandwiches
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tomato Sandwiches
entries.notes: Genius to combine pan con tomate into a tomato sandwich.  One of those recipes where one wonders "why didn't I think of that?"  But all the years of making con tomate and tomato sandwiches both and I didn't until now.  My preference is to leave the mayo off.  Sacrilege perhaps but I really don't like mayo.  It is a great sandwich without mayo or bacon.  But...oh...those onions...nice, sweet summer onions are essential!  Another great Melissa Clark recipe.

entries.title: Roasted Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Tomatoes
entries.notes: Good way to roast garden grown in-season tomatoes that are just on the cusp of being too ripe to save them as delicious somethings as well as the medium ripe winter tomatoes from market to transform them into something delicious.  Textures at end of cook time will be different for one v. the other.  Either a good choice for a buffet service meal any time of year since they are as good room temperature as warm.  "Travel" well too since can be made in advance and reheated.

entries.title: Beans And Garlic Toast In Broth
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Beans And Garlic Toast In Broth
entries.notes: Made as written but for subbing thyme for marjoram.  Delicious dish we enjoy for B, L, or D.  Well, if for B holding garlic rub on the toast if going to work or school.  Otherwise, full rub ahead for that!  I often make parmesan broth to have on hand in freezer and always cook beans from scratch but had not thought to cook the beans with the parm rinds in this way before.  Used Great Northerns and they were done in about 1 1/2 hr.  Also, next time I'd simmer with lid off the pot.  Keeper.

entries.title: Nectarine Raspberry Cobbler With Ginger Biscuits
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Nectarine Raspberry Cobbler With Ginger Biscuits
entries.notes:
Biscuits delightful baked on their own & perfect with coffee or tea.  Take @ 16 min to done on parchment lined sheet @ 375F. Made per recipe for ingredients using gram weights where provided & 1/2&1/2 for liquid & demerara sugar for final sprinkle.  Next time will double pistachios.  Be sure dice to ginger very small. Given eggs in mix & relatively small amount of butter to flour these are 'cake-ier' than most biscuits. See second note for technique comments.

See previous note for ingredient comments.  Delicous biscuits in cobbler or baked on own. Re technique, you definitely want to have these 3/4" at start, less and they won't rise tall enuf.  They expand both ways baking. Using 3" biscuit cutter, best I could get was 9 not 12.  If need 12, use 2.75 or 2.5" cutter.  Do not make them flatter to get there.  Don't overhandle dough - knead only a few turns, not 1 min.  Patting out not rolling out is best in my experience.

entries.title: Spinach Artichoke Dip
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spinach Artichoke Dip
entries.notes: If you're the invitee to "please bring..." events with rep for contributing 'gourmet' each time, bring this & play with them.  After they stop "1995 wants it's food back" & start eating they will say "ahhh…" some.  :-)  Good hot thru lukewarm so fits in buffet spread.  Fine chop spinach MEANS fine. FRESH mozzarella not the plastic stuff.  Try Kim Severson's Bits & Pieces cheese ball for similar 'audience response'. :-)

entries.title: Panzanella With Mozzarella And Herbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Panzanella With Mozzarella And Herbs
entries.notes: Have made a lot of panzanella over the years seldom using a recipe.  Used the recipe for this one and it was a winner with late summer tomatoes.  Only changes were for our palates just a splash more vinegar in step 3 with a skoosh less EVOO and doubling capers.  We started with day-old baguette cubes then oven toasted per step 1 for 10'ish' minutes.  Bread cubes pretty crispy firm by the time the dish went together and we found an hour was the just right 'sit time' in step 4.

entries.title: Angel Hair Pasta With Peppers And Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Angel Hair Pasta With Peppers And Tomatoes
entries.notes: Lusciously seasonal. Just perfect made as written.  The only modification was to grate garlic on microplane rather than smashing to paste.  Used spaghettini as pasta option and ricotta salata for cheese choice.  For those who suggested roasting peppers first - I wouldn't do that but note the finely diced instructions.  It makes all the difference.

entries.title: Rawia Bisharas Brussels Sprouts With Tahini Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Rawia Bisharas Brussels Sprouts With Tahini Sauce
entries.notes:
Delicious.
1)Used oven roast option for cooking sprouts.
2) Opted not to use bread crumbs.
3) Prefer a little more lemon juice than 1/2 C and a bit more pomegranate molasses (just taste until tartness reaches your palate's preference).
4)I think grating garlic on microplane works better than chopping it.  (Thanks to Melissa Clark for that grating of garlic tip years ago that I now use for nearly everything called for chopped.)
5)Good tahini (look in Middle East markets) essential.

entries.title: Lebanese Style Bread Salad With Tomatoes And Herbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lebanese Style Bread Salad With Tomatoes And Herbs
entries.notes: For folks who posted being critical that this recipe is not authentic Fattoush (be it Lebanese, Palestinian or Israeli), for the use of this ingredient or absence of that one, note the author didn't represent it as such.  It is titled "Lebanese-style".  Made as written, this is easy and delicious way to enjoy the bounty of late summer veg.  I prefer to oven toast the pita and sometimes use Arabic flatbread from a local Iraqi market instead of pita.  Don't skip the sumac.

entries.title: Brined Pork Chops With Fennel
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Brined Pork Chops With Fennel
entries.notes: Noting the lemon wedges in the photo for this dish, were they added by food stylist for color contrast interest in the photo, or was there lemon used in recipe but omitted from the text when printed?   A curious cook who plans to make this next week would find it helpful to know. ;-)

entries.title: Creamy Stovetop Corn With Poblano Chiles
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Creamy Stovetop Corn With Poblano Chiles
entries.notes: Great use for late season corn and peak season poblanos and splendid outcome for dish as recipe was written but for using only about half of crème fraiche specified since that's what I had on hand.  Actually seemed just right for finished dish.   Think full fat Greek yogurt or sour cream could be subbed too.  3 ears of corn produced a generous 3 cups of kernels for us.  We used the optional jalapenos.  The lime juice at end "is it" for kiss of acid.  Keeper!

entries.title: Eggplant Caponata
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Eggplant Caponata
entries.notes: Made as written.  Nice agrodolce and while my fully Sicilian hubby argues this may not have been considered caponata in his nonna's kitchen for limited tomato, it is a good eggplant dish.  Olives are important - don't use pimento stuffed variety - we used. Castelvetrano.   My "oops' in misreading recipe was to blanch celery stalks then chop them.  Worked well in spite of my myopia.  Brilliant idea to blanch celery - I'll use technique in other recipes.

entries.title: Tomato Salad On Cumin Spiced Yogurt
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tomato Salad On Cumin Spiced Yogurt
entries.notes: Our market had lovely late season cherry tomatoes in a couple of colors so used those to make this plus newly harvested dill and both greens and whites of scallions.  Delicious flavors in the yogurt and vinaigrette.  (Note recipe was edited after first publication for clarification on how much yogurt goes into the vinaigrette.)  Cool the cumin seeds before grinding.  Used California Ranch "Everyday" Olive Oil.  Jacobsen's Flake Salt used to finish "is it".

entries.title: Chicken Noodle Salad With Creamy Sesame Dressing
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chicken Noodle Salad With Creamy Sesame Dressing
entries.notes:
1) Takes very little effort to make and delivers very tasty outcome.  Even less effort if using leftover roasted chicken breast for 'school night meal' and not poaching 'from scratch'.
2) Tahini brand matters here - look for Alwadi or Soom or similar.
3) Thought dressing benefitted from a little sweetener - sugar or brown sugar or a little honey.
3) Prefer to grate ginger on microplane.
4) Prefer water over broth in the option for those ingredients.

entries.title: Meatloaf Parmesan
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Meatloaf Parmesan
entries.notes:
Re this in my previous notes: "2) Sauce - per recipe. But didn't pour all of it over.".

The reason was I was pretty sure the baking loaf would throw off more fat & protein "ooze"  than I'd want to use as 'spoon over'.  That turned out to be so.  The sauce is essential in baking this, but for us the decision to leave some of it to heat and serve separately was the right one. Discarded most of sauce in pan.

As fun to make as it is tasty. Made per recipe for most part.
1) Loaf - a) used broccoli rabe & chose turkey (ground breast) for second meat.  Next time I'd reduce turkey to 1/2# & use 1 1/2# pork.  b) redux salt to 1/2 tsp - cheese is salty. c) omitted bay leaves from top. d) some say omit olives but I used Castelvetranos & we liked them here.
2) Sauce - per recipe. But didn't pour all of it over.
3) Topping - subbed parm for pecorino.
4) Bake - to 160 degrees internal.

entries.title: Penne With Brussels Sprouts Chile And Pancetta
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Penne With Brussels Sprouts Chile And Pancetta
entries.notes:
Simple and delicious.  Keeper. Made two times.  First per recipe.  Second per recipe but nearly doubled the brussels sprouts - keeping other ingredients per recipe - and didn't use penne.  Both times squeezed juice from half lemon over and tossed well.  If veg doubled then it works well as side dish.  Without penne the brussels mixture is good as bruschetta or crostini topping. (I like to spread bread with soft goat cheese or ewe's cheese first.)

Keeper!
1)The squeeze of lemon juice at end "is it" for finishing touch of acid on this simple and delicious dish.
2) We omitted rosemary.
3)Used pancetta but if you need to sub bacon use unsmoked variety.
4)Recipe serves 2.  Hubby and I find 5.5 oz pasta is plenty for us to each have good sized serving with right ratio of pasta to 8 oz Brussels  for our preferences.
5)Save a little of pasta cookng water in case you want to add at end.

entries.title: Fresh And Wild Mushroom Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Fresh And Wild Mushroom Stew
entries.notes:
Made per recipe but for using 1# oyster mushrooms for pale-wild & 1# cremini for brown.. Relatively little effort needed - especially given make ahead portion  -  for delicious dish.
1)Served with pappardelle and found 12 oz pasta good amount as ragu partner.
2) Used homemade veg broth.
3) Used oyster mushrooms for pale-wild option. Thought they got a tad lost in this.  Next time I'll mix shitake with cremini for browns and use chanterelles for wilds as they might hold up better.

entries.title: Pearl Couscous With Shrimp And Clams
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pearl Couscous With Shrimp And Clams
entries.notes:
Pernod is delightful playmate for shellfish.  But be cautious of using "or other similar anise liquer". Because Anisette & so on will be much too sweet. (Ex: Pernod has 1.8 T sugar/C & anise liquers up to 6T.)  See link here to Pernod 101 by food scientist Harold Magee.  Pernod really has no substitute.  I use it in cooking & agree with Prof. Magee "Pernod is best treated gently." See his #5 point.
https://www.starchefs.com/pernod/html/pernod_101.shtml

entries.title: Sauteed Chicken Breasts With Tarragon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sauteed Chicken Breasts With Tarragon
entries.notes: Curry, paprika and tarragon flavors combined were delightful surprise for me and equally wonderful - or maybe more - is the article on Thomas Keller and his father that accompanies the recipe.  Use "What the last meal taught him" link to read it and appreciate even more the gift that is cooking good food for loved ones - or oneself.  1) Finding 6oz minus breasts a challenge. 2) Chicken may take longer than 1-1.5 min to brown. 3) Add mixed greens w/vinaigrette + crusty bread = dinner.

entries.title: Sweet Potatoes With Bourbon And Brown Sugar
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sweet Potatoes With Bourbon And Brown Sugar
entries.notes: Being among "those" who wonder why people post about recipes they have not yet made, I become one of "those" to do that thing.  To say it was ingredients that drew me in.  What's not to like for in a household where we eat lots of sweets?  Not to mention Thanksgiving on the horizon. But what leads me to post now is recommend "WATCH THE VIDEO!"  With that emphasis.  Ina and Jeffrey?  I'll raise you Melissa and Daniel!  And adorable Dahlia too!

entries.title: Brioche Chestnut Stuffing
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Brioche Chestnut Stuffing
entries.notes: WATCH THE VIDEO. :-)  Melissa, husband Daniel, daughter Dahlia and mom Rita all are delightful.  I send wishes for peace of spirit to this family who will spend their first Thanksgiving without Melissa's father, Rita's husband, this year as Melissa shares in another video.  Rehearsal run of this recipe - which will debut on our TDay table in full form - cut in half (for 4) and using stale Boule I had on hand instead of Brioche was delicious.  Bread torn 1" is key technique here.

entries.title: Pork Stew With Pears And Sweet Potatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pork Stew With Pears And Sweet Potatoes
entries.notes:
Delicious.  Easy.  As written except minor adjustments noted.  
1) 2.5 lb boneless country ribs (a great cut) - well trimmed of fat to start = no extra fat at end
2) Reduced allspice and caraway by half
3) Used 1C wine + 2C water (instead of stock)
4) Used yams (orange) not sweet potatoes (white)
5) Added yams after 1 hr.  Then 30 min more added pears for 15 min.  Just right textures, in particular if planning to reheat.
6) Lemon juice at end + grainy mustard in bowls 'makes it'.

entries.title: Roasted Sweet Potatoes With Hot Honey Browned Butter
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Sweet Potatoes With Hot Honey Browned Butter
entries.notes: My husband is a fiend for orange flesh sweet potatoes (aren't they yams?) and we eat a lot of them in our home, on their own and as part of other dishes.  This stand alone dish for 'sweets'  has become a favorite preparation for a simply easy and delicious meal.  We most often use apple cider vinegar with this, but Rancho Gordo has a pineapple vinegar (no kidding) that is a lovely, low acid vinegar that we like in this recipe too.  And good butter is key.  We like Kerrygold.

entries.title: Baked Egg With Prosciutto And Tomato
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked Egg With Prosciutto And Tomato
entries.notes: Being among the odd-ducks who like egg yolks more firm, my hubby and I find 18-20 minutes is right cook time for us at 375 temp.  And we were reminded last night what a versatile technique is Bittman's baked egg recipe as guideline as we made delightful dinner using leftovers - a little cream in bottom of ramekins, a sprinkle of diced ham, a couple of eggs (in each of the 5" ramekins), topped with  bread crumbs and grated cheese.  Make toast and sip wine as eggs bake.  Ahh-some!

entries.title: Maple Roasted Brussels Sprouts With Toasted Hazelnuts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Maple Roasted Brussels Sprouts With Toasted Hazelnuts
entries.notes: As written for ingredients and oven temp.  Outcome just OK - not a keeper.  15 min cut side down & already tender.  Then cut side up for maple syrup drizzle and done in another 15.  Would have been far overcooked at recipe specified time.  No caramelization so left in and turned up oven for another 10 min but still not.  Good flavor.  But not a stndout for us.

entries.title: Cranberry Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Cranberry Sauce
entries.notes: One of our fav preparations for cranberry sauce, a condiment we eat all season long with lots of things.  We use a bit less OJ and cut the sugar by half only because we prefer cranberry sauce very tart and thick.  Play with the sweetness level for what works for your palate.  The flavor of orange and cranberry is such a good pairing.  Cranberry harvests  in 2018 were so robust that farmers dumped 25% of crops in some states.  Eat an extra helping of this cranberry sauce and help boost sales!

entries.title: Pickled Mushroom Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pickled Mushroom Salad
entries.notes: Made as written for opening course at Thanksgiving.  It was a hit and nary a mushroom left on plates. Were I making this again, I wouldn't change a thing.  Some say reduce sugar, but we all found the agrodolce character very good.  The recipe says it serves 4 but we could have arranged a 5th serving out of it if needed.  I made it in a.m. for dinner service and the roughly 7 hours of marinade time worked.  I used cremini, trumpet and shitake as mushrooms.

entries.title: French Onion Panade
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: French Onion Panade
entries.notes: The woman across the dinner table was a psychologist.  But for the 3 glasses of wine consumed she may not have asked, 'HOW do you stay SO well adjusted given your diagnosis?".  This during course 3 as I mentioned living with progressive CNS disease for 40 years.   "Cooking and food", I said.  Specifically this kind of food and cooking.  Making this kind of dish is Zen-time for me.  And in this recipe it is as if I heard Samin's voice in my kitchen with me.  So delicious!

entries.title: Serious Turkey Stock
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Serious Turkey Stock
entries.notes: Roasting turkey parts delivers rich flavor & great color broth.  Used 4# turkey wings, parsley in place of thyme & 'some' whole black peppercorns instead of ground.  Roasted turkey in roughly 12"w x 8"h stockpot (took 1 hr to get golden), just moving it from oven to stove top at step 2.  Added onions, bay leaves & peppercorns in step 2, simmered 1 hr then added rest of the veg at step 3 & simmered 1 1/2 hr more.  Yield after straining = @ 2qt +3C.

entries.title: Orange Almond Mandelbrot
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Orange Almond Mandelbrot
entries.notes: Made this as written.  It went together exactly as described.  Looked just like the photos at outcome.  And these are delicious in flavor and texture.  Used the flour option (not matzoh meal).  Where volume measures and gram weights were provided I used gram weights.  Only tricky part was shaping logs in step 3.  The recipe takes a little time to do - but some of that is unattended time - but isn't difficult to make.  Wouldn't change a thing.

entries.title: Chinese Chili
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chinese Chili
entries.notes: Does anyone know if I can substitute chuck roast or blade roast trimmed and cut 1/2" dice as per brisket instructions and get the tender outcome desired for this chile in the same simmering time?

entries.title: Sabzi Polo Herbed Rice With Tahdig
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sabzi Polo Herbed Rice With Tahdig
entries.notes: Virgin voyage at making Persian rice for tahdig crust, which we love but had not tried at home, was a great success thanks to Samin's solid instructions (such a good teacher) and a 10" Scanpan for non stick and good browning of crisp tahdig layer.  We used these instructions for rice amounts and technique and timing.  But omitted the herbs to make a saffron rice layer at the bottom using the saffron 'tea' instructions from Samin's Salt-Fat-Acid-Heat book's rice recipe.  Perfect result.

entries.title: Sauteed Scallops With Crushed Peppercorns
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sauteed Scallops With Crushed Peppercorns
entries.notes: Perfect as written!  CenterPoint of Christmas Eve dinner at which my husband's tradition is to eat fish.  (Seven Fishes for many years.  More realistically one these days. :-) )  But for thinking we'd  cook the scallops 6 minutes on each side next time (just our preference for how done we like scallops at center), we wouldn't change a thing.

entries.title: Bits And Pieces Party Cheese Ball
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Bits And Pieces Party Cheese Ball
entries.notes: Good to have tried.  David Lebovitz (Chez Panisse alum of L'Appart and other cookbook fame) starts with citing the same Vivian Howard recipe as referenced for this one.  Roughly the same but without fish sauce (using 1/4 tsp salt and hot sauce instead) and adding 1/4 C chopped dates (and also suggests other dried fruits like apricots or cranberries would work) and a couple TBSP chopped scallions or chives.

entries.title: Clementine Pomegranate Jello Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Clementine Pomegranate Jello Salad
entries.notes: Recipe uses 4 1/2C liquid (juice + water) & 3 packets powdered gelatin.  Assuming Knox (most common brand) their recommendation is 2 C liquid / 1 packet.  For those following recipe & reporting too thick outcome, consider removing 1/2 C water for 4C liquid total & use 2 packets gelatin.  With that ratio, mine turned out of bowl mold just right & lightly held 'wiggly' shape with soft texture on tongue.  Reducing water also makes more juice tart mitigating what some said was not as intense flavor.

entries.title: Persian Spiced Lamb Shanks
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Persian Spiced Lamb Shanks
entries.notes:
*Pretty basic cooking techniques involved.  Tasty.  But so many recipes & so little time - won't do again. 
*Full flavors - for those who thought too bland, wonder if really fresh spices used?
*Reduced water by 1.5 C given some comments -  seemed good to have done.
*Didn't use rose water or buds since we are not fond of them.
*Definitely make this far enough ahead to well defat braise liquid. 
*Needed kiss of sweet at end to balance lime juice in braise liquid - added @1T honey w/ good result

entries.title: Torn Croutons
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Torn Croutons
entries.notes: First, the olive oil measure in this recipe should be 1/3 C.  Looks like a typo in ingredients as published here.  I have been making my own crouton for 30 years, but it didn't occur to me to tear the bread until seeing Samin's recipe.  Genius.  I've made these in both standard and "classic" - see Tip at bottom of recipe - and they're  great to use in salad, with soup, to dip in hummus or other spreads or on their own with a libation.  Airtight container and they've held well for a week for us.

entries.title: Orecchiette With Nduja Shrimp And Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Orecchiette With Nduja Shrimp And Tomatoes
entries.notes: Seems author intends raw broccoli rabe (rapini) to be cooked about 3 min. as part of step 7.  Broccoli rabe (turnip family) is one of few green veg that benefits from long cooking.  Too crunchy and far too bitter as spec'd here.  Cook it first.  (Italian hubby so we use rapini regularly.)  For good tutorial see Samin's Nosrat's Salt-Fat-Acid-Heat or any recipe here by her using rapini.  Or, Viola Butoni's method to cook rapini to use in any dish. https://www.huffpost.com/entry/rapini_b_4072299

entries.title: Lamb Tagine
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lamb Tagine
entries.notes:
1. Made per recipe with following exceptions.  Delicious and just as expected for tagine for form and sauce.  Keeper.

2. Exceptions to as written recipe:  a) Used water instead of stock.  b) Used Emile Henry Dutch Oven which has 'dimpled' lid so didn't need foil and oven at 300 degrees (not 325) for perfectly tender in 2'ish hours.

3. Toasted almonds and other garnish 'make it' for final presentation.

4. Used Calif. not Turkish apricots.  CA cots a bit more tart so didn't need lemon at end.

entries.title: Han Oak Galbijjim
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Han Oak Galbijjim
entries.notes: For the multiple folks who are objecting to the use of Coca Cola in this dish, I'm wondering if that could be much concern about nothing.  First, it is but 2 oz cola per serving.  Also, you aren't eating this three meals x seven a week.  It is one dish for one meal in what I assume wouldn't be more on rotation than weekly.  If that.  Not to mention there is cane sugar sweetened Coke on the market these days.   A couple of oz of cola consumed is not fatal.  IMHO

entries.title: Radish Sandwiches With Butter And Salt
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Radish Sandwiches With Butter And Salt
entries.notes: Very good as written.  For my Italian hubby who never has learned to enjoy butter as bread spread, we also like this with an Israeli version twist.  That is substitute labneh (spreadable consistency yogurt cheese) for the butter.  Then sprinkle with a little sumac and flake salt, add the radishes.  Flat leaf parsley instead of arugula and chives for herbs.  Another riff on this very good starting recipe.  Both are great for appetizers or even for breakfast on the run.

entries.title: Cold Sesame Noodles With Crunchy Vegetables
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Cold Sesame Noodles With Crunchy Vegetables
entries.notes:
As most Bittman recipes, this is very good as written & great to use as inspiration to modify as you choose. Comments:
1) Blending tahini with peanut butter (@ 6T tahini + 2T pb) will get flavor more like Chinese sesame paste.
2) Prefer eensie less sesame oil & a lot more fresh ginger..
3) Prefer a little less soy then add (Diamond Crystal) kosher salt as needed to taste.
5) Rancho Gordo's pineapple vinegar (low acid) is great here but so is rice vinegar.
6) Like to finish w/cilantro.

entries.title: Robertas Pizza Dough
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Robertas Pizza Dough
entries.notes: Hubby grew up in Italian-American neighborhood in Brooklyn with grandmother who spoke only Italian so he not considers himself 'expert' (in jest) on good pizza crust &  makes a lot of pizza at home.  He said this was one of the easiest to work with doughs he has ever used.  The chew & taste were great!  A couple of notes:  1) We weigh everything but 8g salt (Diamond Crystal) was over 1.5t.  Used 1t.  2) Watch the video in addition to reading recipe.  3) Did second rise at room temp for 4.5 hrs.

entries.title: Sour Cream And Onion Dip
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sour Cream And Onion Dip
entries.notes:
Easy recipe.  Tasty outcome.  Notes  to share:
1) Taking time to caramelize onions is key -  they don't need babysitting but do need attending.  Took @ 1hr 15 min.
2) Some say 'too sweet' but caramelized onions are that.  As expected in that regard &  "skoosh" of lemon juice added in pan at end balances that before chopping.
3) Make ahead seemed a good idea BUT thickened from dip to spread consistency in fridge overnight.  Day of use assembly might be best.
4) Didn't need EVOO drizzle at end.

entries.title: Lentils With Chorizo Greens And Yellow Rice
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lentils With Chorizo Greens And Yellow Rice
entries.notes:
Rustic. Comforting. Delicious. Easy. Keeper.
1. Cut in half - served 3 generously. Looked like pix.
2. Made as written for ingredients except subbed escarole, had on hand, for kale.
3. Great flavors in lentils & in rice. Tread lightly adding the extra salt to  lentils in step 3 - Chorizo releases salt as it simmers.
4. Noting several reported problems with rice, I note step 7 doesn't say specifically to cover rice for the 15 min. of cook.  I did. Then left covered for 25 min. steam.  Perfect

entries.title: Fettuccine With Ricotta And A Fistful Of Mint
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Fettuccine With Ricotta And A Fistful Of Mint
entries.notes: In the same theme as Dorie Greenspan's Spoonable Ricotta (Everyday Dorie), this rendition is really tasty too.  Not just for pasta but to top potatoes.  (I'm thinking this will be particularly good with new potatoes and Spring peas in market in a few weeks.)  It is also yummy spread on toast.  (Ask me how I know that.)   Or as dip for veggies.  Or to spread on crackers.  Or to eat with a spoon.  A good quality ricotta, like Bel Gioioso Con Latte, works as well in reduced fat as whole milk.

entries.title: Champagne Cocktail
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Champagne Cocktail
entries.notes: Luscious.  Keeper!  Made to sip while cooking Valentine's Day dinner to celebrate 40 Valentine's Days shared in the kitchen together and acknowledge the bitter that comes with the sweet in love.  Corny - sure - but it's what caught our eye to try this libation.  This was a hit with both of us!.  Notable since my hubby does not share my affection for French 75 which I enjoy as champagne cocktail.  We used dry Prosecco and orange bitters and dialed back the bitters just a bit.

entries.title: Mussakhan Roast Chicken With Sumac And Red Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mussakhan Roast Chicken With Sumac And Red Onions
entries.notes: Made recipe as written.  Good enough to be glad I tried it.  Not so good as to make it to 'keeper' .  I have had other versions of Mussakhan in which  the chicken is pulled off the bone to top the naan and I prefer that presentation.  Also, this seemed just a tad salty for our palates using the 1 1/2 tsp salt.  (Diamond Crystal Kosher.)  I'd cut that back a bit.  375 oven took about 55 min to done.

entries.title: Mark Bittmans Pasta With Clams
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mark Bittmans Pasta With Clams
entries.notes:
Paul and Lawrence say the parsley is more than a garnish.  So agree with them.  Essential - not much is needed but it is needed and makes a difference.

KCH says use more clams, like the 48, use good ingredients and get out of the way.  Amen!

I sometimes like to add a splash or two of whatever white wine I might be drinking with this into the clam liquid in Step 4.  Sometimes but not always.

This is as good as it gets to perfect.

entries.title: Pasta With Roasted Cauliflower And Blue Cheese
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pasta With Roasted Cauliflower And Blue Cheese
entries.notes:
Delicious and easy.  Worthy of "company's coming" or weeknight supper.
1. Made per recipe for ingredients cutting all in half and using 5 oz pasta to make 2 generous servings.
2. Skipped chives - I was out.  But see #5 for other topping.
3. Fresh lemon juice at end is essential - don't skip it.
4. Also good with peas added along with the other veg - maybe 1C for full recipe.
5. Toasted bread crumbs to top each serving is a nice touch.

entries.title: Sauerkraut And Apples
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sauerkraut And Apples
entries.notes: I made this a couple of times, both as whole recipe and as half, omitting the bacon but all other ingredients and it is delightful in that vegetarian mode.  Don't be tempted to use a vegetable oil, like olive oil, in place of butter.  Something about the butter "makes it" in combination with the rest.  We choose a low IBU (International Bitterness Units) brew and particularly like Belgian Ale used as the beer.   Comes together fast enough to be weeknight meal.

entries.title: Chocolate Mug Cake
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chocolate Mug Cake
entries.notes:
In my previous comment about trying out tahini, " I thought about the question someone asked about using oil instead of butter. I didn't want to try oil (I love butter) but it made me wonder if tahini - a great chocolate partner - would work instead. Used 1TBSP tahini plus a few drops of water in place of butter. Worked very well.", I should have added that it was a good Lebanese pourable consistency tahini.  I like AlWadi.  But any pourable, like SOOM, should work.

Made this 2 times following the recipe but for using salted butter & so eliminating pinch of kosher salt.  Chose 3T for the sugar & was plenty sweet.  Fun & delicous & looked just like photo.  The 3rd time I thought about the question someone asked about using oil instead of butter.  I didn't want to try oil (I love butter) but it made me wonder if tahini - a great chocolate partner - would work instead.  Used 1TBSP tahini plus a few drops of water in place of butter.  Worked very well.

How fun this was to make!  We used salted butter (Kerrygold) so skipped the pinch of kosher salt.  Otherwise made as written and opted for 3 TBSP sugar which was plenty sweet.  I bet this would be yummy with a tiny scoop of vanilla ice cream but alas none in the house.  This is a perfect little recipe to introduce kids to cooking or to bring out he kid in we...old...umm...well seasoned cooks who love to play in the kitchen.

entries.title: Ashure Cereal
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Ashure Cereal
entries.notes:
Addictively delicious.  Notes based on making twice:
1) Used gram weights for all where it was noted.
2) Second time reduced honey & brown sugar to 100g each - prefer that.
3) Mixed spices in with sesame seeds before mixing into nuts & kamut - worked well to distribute spices throughout.
4) Bake time seems best at 7 min for 1st bake & 5 min for 2nd.
5) We make for snacking not as breakfast cereal so we sprinkle a little flake salt over when trays come out of oven

For those looking for mahaleb, there really is no substitute for this, and if your local Middle Eastern market is out of it (as mine), thought it might be useful to know this is sometimes spelled mahleb if you're doing a web search.  World Spice is out of stock.  Savory Spice no longer sells it.  But SNUK has some available.  Buy the smallest amount you can.  A little goes a long way and too much can sometimes add bitterness.

For those asking about puffed grains availability and about puffed grains to sub for wheat to make gluten free dish, thought this might be helpful.  You can find puffed wheat pretty readily all grocery stores that have a natural foods section.  Arrowood is one brand broadly distributed and makes puffed grains sold in bags.  Wheat.  Brown Rice (GF).  Kamut (ancient wheat derivative and not GF.)  Also it changes the texture a bit but puffed Millet - grass not grain so GF might be worth a try.

entries.title: Red Bean Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Red Bean Stew
entries.notes:
Nice outcome which may not be "wow" flavors outcome that some are looking for based on others' comments but a good "yum" bowl.  Good day one, better day two, freezes and reheats well.  Made per recipe except for the following changes:
1) Drained soaking water and added all fresh water to beans in the pot.
2) Used Rancho Gordo's pineapple vinegar (a favorite in bean dishes) instead of red wine vinegar.
3) Used only fresh dill at end and about 1/4 C since I was out of parsley.

entries.title: Mark Araxs Marinated Lamb
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mark Araxs Marinated Lamb
entries.notes: I'll echo Nan's comment.  Don't be fooled that such a simple recipe can deliver such simply delicious results.  Keeper!

entries.title: Pea Dip With Parmesan
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pea Dip With Parmesan
entries.notes:
A favorite at our house.  First time I made this I pureed all the peas, didn't leave any out to stir in whole.  It was delicious.  Each time after that I made it as written to leave out a cup of peas to stir in later.  Even more delicious.  We like a little more mint and a little less garlic than specified.  What makes this even better is that peas are a nutrition powerhouse for protein and fiber both.  Tastes great and is good for you.

Delicious to eat, simple to make, pretty color to serve and, like all Bittman recipes, versatile.  Prefer putting all peas in food processor / blender for texture.  Eliminated pine nuts.  (Can't use them.)  Great with mint but if out can also sub a couple tsp. fresh thyme and crushed red pepper (Calabrian or Aleppo are nice) for a flavor twist.  Like to serve on crostini.  Added bonus - peas are nutrition powerhouse for antioxidants & protein (8g/C.).  What's not to love?

entries.title: Lemony Whipped Feta With Charred Scallions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemony Whipped Feta With Charred Scallions
entries.notes:
1.  Keeper. Made as written in morning of event day & refrigerated until @ 1 hr  B4 service.  Texture just right.

2. I sliced  lemons very thin for 2 benefits.  a) They'll char at same time as scallions. b) Tucked in  across the spread they are easy to spoon out with the spread & delicious to eat.

3. Labneh, Fromage Blanc or Greek Yogurt can sub for cream cheese - will be more 'tang' & lighter texture if so.

4.  See NYT Cooking demo video here.  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=galpmZHzwIs

entries.title: Red Curry Mussels And Roasted Sweet Potatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Red Curry Mussels And Roasted Sweet Potatoes
entries.notes: Makrut lime leaves are also called kaffir lime leaves and looking for them labeled as the latter may make it easier to find them.  Even in some mainstream grocers - typically in the fresh herb produce section.  Easier to buy if you have a SE Asian or Indian market nearby.  Kaffir lime leaves add a distinct quality to a dish and I use a number of recipes that call for them.  The good news is if you do find them, they freeze well to use as you need them.

entries.title: Chopped Salad With Apples Walnuts And Bitter Lettuces
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chopped Salad With Apples Walnuts And Bitter Lettuces
entries.notes:
Delicious keeper we made multiple times in Fall and Winter when heirloom apples were plentiful. Notes:

1) Some pears work here too but the outcome isn't as tart as choosing the right apples.

2) Just for personal preference for the amount of acid we prefer in salad dressings, we cut back on the oil a bit to increase the acids to fats balance.

3) We prefer to grate the garlic with a microplane or mash it up on the cutting board with a little salt to make it into a paste before adding

entries.title: Barbecue Country Style Pork Ribs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Barbecue Country Style Pork Ribs
entries.notes:
Top notch for flavors & ease of prep.  Keeper. Notes:

1) Used boneless country style ribs (great cut - 'introduced' by NYT article last year & now a fav). 
 
2) Ingredients as recipe - didn't use optional chiles.

3) Procedure - a) Salted pork 2 hr ahead.  b) After adding water brought to simmer on cooktop then covered into 300 oven @ 1hr + uncovered 1 1/4 hr
 
4) Throws LOTs of fat.  Recommend separating meat from broth at end & chill overnight to remove big fat cap then put meat in to reheat.

entries.title: Roasted Garlic And White Bean Dip With Rosemary
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Garlic And White Bean Dip With Rosemary
entries.notes:
Intro'd to warm white bean dip w/rosemary, like this, at Denver Italian eatery that served w/ grissini.   I serve this the same way.  Notes:

*Best served warm.
*Great w / glass of any kind of vino.
*Much prefer to start with beans I cook myself rather than canned.  (Home cooked beans freeze well or hold in fridge for days to use when needed.)
*Also fine w/out fried rosemary to finish. Use olive oil.
*Can replace cayenne w / paprika (for color) to garnish if serving heat averse.

entries.title: Mexican Rice
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mexican Rice
entries.notes: Made per recipe choosing water for water / stock option and using very well rinsed rice (swishing in a couple - three of bowls of water and draining until water is clear-ish) it turned out just right for flavor and the 17 min cook / 10 min stand for time was perfect for being done.  Keeper.

entries.title: Sheet Pan Pizza With Asparagus And Arugula
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sheet Pan Pizza With Asparagus And Arugula
entries.notes:
Made per recipe with 2 small tweaks:

1) cut asparagus in 1-inch pieces not 5-inch pieces as recipe specified

and

2) used prosciutto instead of pancetta and given the nature of prosciutto added it not in step 2 but at the same time as olives in Step 3.  

Otherwise, per recipe for ingredients and instructions.  Just right for amount of topping.  It was done just right at the temperature and in the time called for.  

Cut in squares to serve.  Good warm and also tasty at room temperature

entries.title: Fettuccine With Merguez And Mint Pesto
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Fettuccine With Merguez And Mint Pesto
entries.notes:
Excellent outcome.  We prepared it mostly as written.  All ingredients we love but in this dish the mint pesto 'makes it'.    Only deviations:  
* We cut the Kalamata's in half
* Used regular, not spinach, fettucine
* Instead of sun dried tomatoes we used large cherry tomatoes, cut in quarters and tossed in at end right before serving.

entries.title: Eggs With Gigante Beans And Harissa
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Eggs With Gigante Beans And Harissa
entries.notes: Made recipe as written choosing prosciutto instead of mojama and one egg, not two, per serving.  All were surprised how good a pairing was egg with this as some were skeptical at start.  Delicous all around.  One diner with egg allergy thought the workaround of another dollop of crème fraiche on top to fill in for fat of egg yolk was delicious that way too.  PS - I always start with fresh cooked beans, not canned.  Cooking ahead and refrigerating or freezing keeps them handy.

entries.title: Pork And Ricotta Meatballs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pork And Ricotta Meatballs
entries.notes:
1. Really versatile meatballs that can be added to marinara or in broths or for sandwiches.  That they freeze well makes them easy to have on hand. 

2. Made recipe as written except for cutting back on salt - used maybe 1/2 tsp - due to parmesan.  Cooked a bit to taste for before forming meatballs and less salt was just right for our taste buds.

3. Used homemade bread crumbs - if using commercial product be sure they are unseasoned/salted.  

4. Took a little longer in oven than 15 min.

Out of room in previous comments to add that the pork mixture is easier to handle to form the meatballs if you have the time to cover it and put in in the fridge for 30 - 60 minutes first.  Just that little bit of time to let the mixture 'rest' makes a difference.

entries.title: Simple Braised Potatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Simple Braised Potatoes
entries.notes: Yum!  What if?  Typical responses from my husband and me when reading a Bittman recipe.  We cook 5 1/2  of 7 days a week.  We have been cooking - as kids - since Johnson was President.  In all those years we have never braised potatoes like this. Until now.  How is it we were missing out on what now may be our fav technique?  In  'what if' mode, we had a little schmaltz to use up so subbed that for butter, water with a splash or two of Belgian style ale for broth with fresh thyme.  Yumm.

entries.title: Sablefish With Hazelnut Crust
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sablefish With Hazelnut Crust
entries.notes: Sablefish, aka butterfish, is a rich fish 'in the nude' and we found the addition of butter and hazelnuts to be just too much to enjoy this dish, which I made per the recipe.  I did like the concept for the wine and miso on the fish before adding the butter toasted nuts to bake and the wine and honey sauce finish.  Those miso and wine - next time using white in each - and honey elements I'll try again with some other fish.  But in mode of different strokes, this was too rich for us.

entries.title: Coconut Rice With Peas
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Coconut Rice With Peas
entries.notes: Cut this in half, it served four nicely, and made the recipe as written with delicous outcome.  Just right on every level for subtle flavors and perfect texture.  Only note I would add is I typically find basmati is done at 17 or 18 minutes and so that is the time I used, not the 20 specified in the recipe.

entries.title: Herb And Radish Salad With Feta And Walnuts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Herb And Radish Salad With Feta And Walnuts
entries.notes: Made as written and cut in half it easily fed three people.  Refreshingly delicious.  IMHO toasting walnuts is key (I note some say they used them raw).  The sieving step was a new tip for me in using walnuts toasted, which I often do.  I'll apply that to other recipes now too.  Top quality sheep's feta is also key here as is olive oil quality, using Persian, not regular garden variety, cukes and really fresh herbs.  Each ingredient supports the others.

entries.title: Sous Vide Rib Steaks With Spicy Salsa Verde
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sous Vide Rib Steaks With Spicy Salsa Verde
entries.notes: In a riff on the old movie line -  "Leave the gun.  Take the cannoli." - even if you don't plan to make the sous vide steak try this recipe for the delicious salsa verde.  It can be drizzled on roasted or grilled veg, on almost any protein, spooned on bread or over fresh tomatoes or maybe just eaten with a spoon!  I prefer a little more lime than specified. A little flake salt - I like Jacobsen's - added over top of whatever you're putting this on is the "just right" end touch.

entries.title: Dandelion Salad With Garlic Confit Dressing
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Dandelion Salad With Garlic Confit Dressing
entries.notes:
Looking for NYT Cooking instructions to confit garlic & came upon this recipe.  (And Marian Burros confit instructions too.)  Delicous and made mostly as written but:

*used big bunch of arugula not dandelion,
*upped lemon juice by a good bit for personal preference,
*reduced 1/4 tsp salt to big pinch which worked given capers add salt.
 
Flake salt to finish is key.

If extra garlic and oil left put them in a sealable glass container in the fridge.  Should hold 2 weeks'ish for lots of uses.

entries.title: Arugula Pesto
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Arugula Pesto
entries.notes: Made per recipe using @ 1/4C EVOO & 1/3C "plus" parmesan.  Made all in food processor.  Great flavor used as spread on ciabbata sandwiches, tossed in pasta w/ fresh tomatoes  & swirled in ricotta as dip for veggies & torn croutons (Samin's).  Arugula is peppery brassicale (like mustard) containing glucosinolate that some people's taste buds process as more bitter.  Noting some say this recipe is bitter, if you find arugula to be so this likely isn’t the pesto for you.

entries.title: Summer Corn Soup
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Summer Corn Soup
entries.notes: Local farm corn is abundant and I've been on a corn soup makin' kick.  This recipe was one of our recent favs for outcome made as written.  With respect to those who used chicken broth or thought it needed same, I think the corn stock here is deliciously perfect on its' own.  We prefer fresh thyme over basil as garnishing herb and use olive not canola oil.  An easy recipe to cut in half if you want to do that but leftovers really good re-heated.  I'm going to try freezing some next time.

entries.title: Fresh Corn And Tomato Soup
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Fresh Corn And Tomato Soup
entries.notes: Made mostly per recipe with delicious outcome.  I've never used cornmeal to thicken corn soup but Bittman seldom steers me wrong so I used it.  Brilliant idea.  Albeit I used half of what was specified (2 T not 1/4 C).  Bob's Red Mill medium grind was what I had on hand and it was just right for this.

entries.title: Blistered Green Beans And Tomatoes With Honey Harissa And Mint
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Blistered Green Beans And Tomatoes With Honey Harissa And Mint
entries.notes: Perhaps the fav at my home for new-to-us green bean recipe this year.  Made as written but for adding an 'eensie' more lime juice.  Used avocado oil - my preference for high smoke point vegetable oil - in place of grapeseed.  Also "know thy harissa" may be a good thought to keep in mind given they can vary so much in heat level.  I used the spiciest of my 'stash' and it worked well.  Pretty - looked exactly like photo.  Good warm and also good room temp if you want to make ahead.

entries.title: Tomato Risotto
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tomato Risotto
entries.notes: Made 2X per recipe for ingredients, proportions & technique.  Splendid.  If reduced by 1/3 all around will serve 3 ample or 2 uber-generous portions.  Used ripe Heirloom tomatoes both times with no need to peel or seed when cut in small dice. Used carnaroli rice - my fav for risottos.  Needed "skoosh" more broth (used parm broth) both times & "2 min. minus" more cook time to just right.  Second time finished with soft butter not EVOO in step 4.  Delicious either way - liked butter best.

entries.title: Rustic Shrimp Bisque
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Rustic Shrimp Bisque
entries.notes:
*Great recipe in particular for people like my husband who enjoy bisque textures but not the dairy usually in them.
*Made this several times all with good outcome.  As deviation from instructions, we prefer using immersion blender to puree soup BEFORE adding in chopped shrimp then proceed to add in shrimp & finish.
*Sub Dungeness crab for shrimp & you'll have a dish similar to Scottish Partan Bree - delicious variation. Use crab shell for stock, crab meat in the bisque.

entries.title: Lamb Flatbread With Zaatar
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lamb Flatbread With Zaatar
entries.notes:
Made dish per recipe.  Delicious keeper. Notes:

*Feta adds salt here & some za'atar mixes have salt in them.  If yours does, be mindful when 'generously' salting onions - step 3.

*Note step 7 instruction - don't over bake.   Pliability when done is key.  You don't want these to be crisp.

* The fresh herbs to garnish are "it".
  
*  Our favorite temperature to eat these is just slightly warm.

entries.title: Marinated Feta With Herbs And Peppercorns
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Marinated Feta With Herbs And Peppercorns
entries.notes:
1) Delicious made per recipe choosing preserved lemon option in the "or orange..." choice, including optional serrano & electing only black / green peppercorns in the "any combination of".

2) Used my preferred firm, brine packed Feta which is French in origin & sheep's milk based.

3) If oil is leftover when cheese is all eaten, it is good for crostini or with pasta or roasted veg or on salads.

4) Presented in a nice glass container, this makes an "ahh"some host(ess) gift.

entries.title: Grilled Corn And Avocado Salad With Feta Dressing
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Grilled Corn And Avocado Salad With Feta Dressing
entries.notes: Made per recipe. Delicious outcome.  Also as idea from this recipe, the feta, buttermilk, lemon zest and juice and garlic can be mashed and whisked at the bottom of a salad bowl as dressing.  Then sturdy greens - like romaine or frisee - on top along with other fridge friendly salad items (sliced radish, onions, corn...) and put in fridge ahead of meal.  When ready for salad course the dressing is ready to go in the bottom of the bowl to toss in.  If using tomatoes toss them in last.

entries.title: Pasta With Zucchini Feta And Fried Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pasta With Zucchini Feta And Fried Lemon
entries.notes: First try of this will be dinner this week where I need something to make in advance & eat room temp.  Reading comments in advance & noting some found the lemon too bitter, see recommendation on 'how to avoid' via Melissa Clark's fantabulous recipe for "Pasta With Fried Lemons and Chile Flakes" on this site.  Watch her VIDEO for sectioning lemons, slicing and blanching before frying them to avoid bitterness.  I will try this recipe as written first time but offer 'heads up' for M. Clark's tips.

entries.title: Chicken Stock
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Chicken Stock
entries.notes:
I would follow Samin anywhere and made this several times.  My 'go to'. 
 Making chicken stock for years but as usual, Samin makes me wiser - better cook and I now add vinegar.  BUT...

*I think this is better if you add only water and chicken to the stockpot, bring to a boil, lower to simmer and let it go about 20 min then skim 'gunk" and THEN add veg (and vinegar).

*I find 4 hrs is plenty of time.  

Note:  Saved fat that rises to top is great for potatoes or root veg cooking.

entries.title: Spicy Caramelized Squash With Lemon And Hazelnuts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Caramelized Squash With Lemon And Hazelnuts
entries.notes:
Simple and delicious.  Made recipe as written but for swapping out the hot smoked paprika with regular (aka sweet) paprika and a little cayenne. 
 Used butternut squash.  Noting others mention their dish burned at the recommended temp and time for cooking, ours was just right in 45 minutes @ 425.  We did turn it twice.  The lemon zest, juice, nuts and flake salt are 'it' for finishing the dish.  If hazelnuts aren't your thing, try almonds or pistachios or pepitas.  Skin was edible texture.

entries.title: Sweet Potato Casserole Pie
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sweet Potato Casserole Pie
entries.notes: Made as written and cut in half using a 6.5" springform pan for 6 servings with great outcome for form and flavor.  Delicious - just sweet enough but not too much. Much prefer this texture over traditional sweet potato pie.  Made it day 1 through step 5, finished it day 2 and easy peasy.  Serving issues were:  a) removing from spring form pan bottom to serving platter a bit difficult, 2) needed to use sharp narrow knife (chose boning knife) to cleanly cut through marshmallow top for slicing.

entries.title: Rice Pilaf With Pumpkin Currants And Pine Nuts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Rice Pilaf With Pumpkin Currants And Pine Nuts
entries.notes:
Fan of Tanis' recipes and in particular rice, but think there may be an error in this that results in what some report as 'crunchy' rice, etc.  That is if using Bomba or Arborio (or Caranoli which is Aborio-type) since rice to liquid ratio for those is 1 part to 3 parts (because they absorb more than a long grain like basmati) you need to use 1C rice here for best outcome.  We used 1 C Carnaroli, the rest of recipe as written except reduced butter by half.   20 min cook + 15 rest = Perfect

Entire dinner in which this recipe appeared will be our Thanksgiving this year.  A note in advance for others regarding a very good source for excellent Turkish variety pine nuts at the on-line shop of Maureen Abood.  I used these in multiple dishes and found them the best.  https://maureenaboodmarket.com/collections/our-best-sellers/products/extra-large-pine-nuts  I had pine-nut-mouth several years using the "bin brand" ones, mostly from China, and I'm now very choosy about pine nuts.

entries.title: Greek Baked Squash Omelet
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Greek Baked Squash Omelet
entries.notes: Having leeks and Sugar Pie variety pumpkin leftover from Thanksgiving produce to use up, this recipe was inspiration for a "what will we have for dinner?" easy to pull together meal.   Subbed the pumpkin for winter squash and since no fresh dill on hand used Italian parsley.   Otherwise per recipe.  Tasty outcome.  Equally good not and just barely warm.

entries.title: One Pot Pasta With Ricotta And Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: One Pot Pasta With Ricotta And Lemon
entries.notes: For whatever reason I'm less inclined to use basil as winter pasta add in and it was early December when I made this lovely dish.  So I used a few torn arugula leaves.  But I think shredded radicchio or torn endive or any other sort of winter chicory would be an equally good choice.  So would Italian parsley.  Otherwise I made this per recipe (using cavatappi as pasta choice) and it was a really delicious dish using winter lemons as 'tis the season for citrus.

entries.title: Thumbprints With Dulce De Leche Nutella Or Jam
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Thumbprints With Dulce De Leche Nutella Or Jam
entries.notes: "Cookies will keep in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 1 week."  Wait!  What did we do wrong?  The pecan with dulce filling disappeared in 24 hours. ;-)  Excellent cookies!

entries.title: Pork Chops In Cherry Pepper Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pork Chops In Cherry Pepper Sauce
entries.notes: Taking a page from Dan G's book when he comments that this dish is delicious and akin to Scarparello which he recommends everyone try, I agree if you enjoy this dish you will likely enjoy that one.  And a recipe published on this site is one of the very best for Scarpariello I have ever had.  See link here.   https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/11990-raos-chicken-scarpariello-shoemakers-chicken

entries.title: Clam Dip
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Clam Dip
entries.notes: Just right for us made per recipe with but one little tweak - used just one big pinch of cayenne and then added a few shakes of Tabasco.  My single edit would be that I'm not sure you'll need the clam liquor if you're using the canned chopped clams.  We found this came together for nicely dip-able texture without it.  And as PS - if using canned clams invest in a really good onshore brand.  This is easy to scale down by half if you're serving a smaller group.

entries.title: Marinated Giant White Beans And Beets
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Marinated Giant White Beans And Beets
entries.notes:
*Made bean portion of this looking for home cooked analog to the marinated large white beans found at antipasti bars at grocery chains (Whole Foods etc), most of which come from same mass distributor and use soybean oil (ugh).

*Made twice - once with Royal Corona beans and then Judion beans, both soaked overnight before cooking.  

*Roasted the red bell pepper & used more of it.  Subbed thyme for dill.  Added splash wine vinegar.

*Holds well for 3/4 days in fridge.  Served room temp.

entries.title: Fatty Cue Brussels Sprouts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Fatty Cue Brussels Sprouts
entries.notes: We loved Brussels sprouts since before doing so was cool and all the ingredients here are things we like so well as to almost always have them on hand.  This combination sounded dee-luscious but fell short for us of being a "keeper".  We made it per recipe but for our taste buds it just didn't click.  If I zero in, it might have been that as much as we love coriander - seeds and ground - the flavor didn't work for us in this.

entries.title: Passover Inspired Braised Lamb With Dried Fruit
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Passover Inspired Braised Lamb With Dried Fruit
entries.notes: Cooking in the time of covid19 to find lamb in freezer and all other ingredients on hand so dinner could be made without a need for non-essential trip to market.  Too early for Passover or Easter by a little but so delicious and comforting for a meal.  The "ahhh...roma" as it cooked alone was enough to raise spirits.  Made as written a day ahead to reheat.   Chose fresh ginger. Apricots broke down a lot to add to 'saucey'.  Would increase prunes next time - yummy. Used cilantro garnish.

entries.title: Cheesy Cauliflower Toasts
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Cheesy Cauliflower Toasts
entries.notes:
Made per recipe.  Very easy.  Very delicious.  Very much improves Ina Garten original - which I also used once.   Notes:

1) The only rub is not specifying cauliflower head size.  So "medium-ish".  You'll know when stirring in the cauliflower to the ricotta mixture when to call 'enough'.  (Roasted cauliflower not used here is great all other ways.)

2) Yes. You DO want to put the nutmeg in this.  Absolutely. 

3) Prefer ricotta in this and specifically Bel Gioso brand Ricotta Con Latte.

entries.title: Green Garlic Toast
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Green Garlic Toast
entries.notes: Our Farmers Market opened even in covid19 times thanks to creative and determined manager and nimble innovative crew of volunteers to pivot to order ahead and drive through to pick up local farmer and producer treasures. (Covid19 has broken some big supply chains but local ones continue to work.)  Which is how I came have green garlic to make this excellent compound butter.  To spread on artisan toasted local baker bread from the market .  And eat with local hot house tomatoes. Ahhhh...some.

entries.title: Spicy Green Garlic Chicken Soup
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Green Garlic Chicken Soup
entries.notes: Green garlic season is fleeting.  Perhaps that it arrives this year in the time of covid19 makes one all the more mindful of how uncertain things can be and wanting to cook while ingredients exist.  So using this spring's green garlic, I made this soup twice.  Once per recipe - used pastured chicken from farmers market.  Once in vegan mode using Royal Corona beans (no joke intended - from Rancho Gordo) as the base so one has bean pot liquor not chicken broth.   Both times very good.  Keeper.

entries.title: Spicy Korean Temple Noodles
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Korean Temple Noodles
entries.notes:
Printed page of this recipe was filed in my 'to try' folder for a year until I did that last night.  Recipe now in 'keeper' binder. 
Super easy.  Very tasty. Made nearly as written but for subbing spaghetti for udon.  instead of cucumber used small dice of orange bells and celery.  Covid19 shopping times meant using what was on hand and  the cool, crispy veg were good partners here.  Benefited from 90 min 'ish' sit at room temp before serving.

entries.title: Macaroni Salad With Lemon And Herbs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Macaroni Salad With Lemon And Herbs
entries.notes: At its' delicious best when chilled for a couple to a few hours.  And delicious it is.  I was tempted to eat the entire dish all by myself.  The 'peel the celery' instruction is a good one.  I always do this for use in cold salads of any kind because it makes a difference - here too.  I'll use grainy mustard next time and no caper brine.  If you're absent buttermilk, I think sour cream could work fine to substitute.  This is now in keep and often repeat file.

entries.title: The Most Adaptable One Bowl Cornmeal Poundcake
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: The Most Adaptable One Bowl Cornmeal Poundcake
entries.notes:
Delightful recipe!  Notes:

1) Per recommendations to reduce sugar, I did that to a scant 3/4C - perfect for us.

2) Otherwise, made per recipe - choosing orange zest & vanilla options -  but with an addition of 1C diced tender rhubarb.  Mixed it wtih 2T of the total sugar to let sit while preparing the rest of cake batter then gently stirred rhubarb into batter.  Very good outcome for it staying suspended in batter. Delicious too.

3) Used 9" pan but 8" would also work.

4) 50'ish' min. bake.

entries.title: Quick Ragu With Ricotta And Lemon
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Quick Ragu With Ricotta And Lemon
entries.notes:
*Ground lamb in place of beef + harissa in place of sambal olek.
*Consider adding Merguez spices - fennel, cumin, coriander  - when sauteing lamb.  Or not.
*Sub a more lamb and hot spice friendly wine - lower tannin, more fruit forward vino red or white - for Cab.  Doesn't need to be spendy but something you would choose to drink with this.
*Consider preserved lemon (peel only of course) to replace fresh lemon zest.
*Otherwise per recipe.  Delicous!

entries.title: Garlic Rasam
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Garlic Rasam
entries.notes: Every item in this recipe (absent curry leaves) is a staple in my kitchen but for the toor dal.  I have red, black, green and brown lentils, green split peas, and umpteen kinds of beans but wouldn't ya know...no pigeon peas / yellow lentil aka toor dal.  Rats.  Rather than run out for 1 tsp I'm going to try red lentil as substitute since I thinks a little thickening is what the toor dal provides.  They will cook faster than the toor dal, I know, but seem the best option of what I have on hand.

entries.title: Eggs Kejriwal
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Eggs Kejriwal
entries.notes: Equally as delicious without the cheese if cooking for folks who can't eat dairy.  Just as perfect for dinner as breakfast or snack.  Thanks for this.

entries.title: Sour Cream And Fruit Scones
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sour Cream And Fruit Scones
entries.notes:
Came together in a snap. Outcome = tender, perfect texture scones. Notes:

*Technique per recipe.  Used gram weights not measures.

*Put cold butter in freezer for @1hr prior to grating.

*Used sour cream & whole milk per recipe.

*Noticing sweetness comments of others, cut back sugar by @1/3rd.  Glad I did.

*Used fresh diced rhubarb for fruit.  Macerated @ 20min w/2T of total sugar.

*Cut 8 triangles not squares.

*Omitted egg to brush top.  Used milk + sprinkle demerara. sugar.

* 19 min bake

entries.title: Spicy Meatballs With Chickpeas
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Meatballs With Chickpeas
entries.notes:
Delicious meatballs & gravy.  (Made w/o chickpeas.) Some say problems w/rice being fully cooked or dry meatballs.  Our outcome great.  What we did:

*Lamb as meat.

*White basmati rice rinsed several times 'til water is clear (how I always handle basmati b4 cooking).

*Meatball ingredients & technique per recipe to combine.

*But browned meatballs in braising pan on cooktop then defatted pan and added 'gravy', covered & low simmer for @30 min.

*Didn't use saffron in gravy - felt it would be los

entries.title: Spicy And Saucy Cherry Tomato Pasta
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy And Saucy Cherry Tomato Pasta
entries.notes:
*A good use for cherry tomatoes at the market or on your kitchen counter which may be an "eensie" towards the downhill side of wanting to munch on them fresh or toss them in a salad.  I usually oven roast those little gems at that point but this recipe offers a perfect alternative to enjoy them.

*The little fried capers were an "it's it!" addition.  

*Depending on how saucy you like pasta you may want to consider 12 oz not 1 lb.

*Any shaped pasta works but good with spaghetti too.

entries.title: Sweet And Sour Cauliflower With Golden Raisins
