entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sweet And Sour Cauliflower With Golden Raisins
entries.notes:
*Made per recipe first time & this was just right agrodolce balance of sweet & tart for us. (Chose turmeric in the '...or saffron' option.)  

*Took a couple of extra min. to cook cauliflower step 2 per notes of others.  But also note this is supposed to be just tender not soft.

*Tasty warm or room temp. Appreciate it can be made ahead.

*Second time subbed Farmers' Market 'new to me' veg Caraflex cabbage (cut @ 1.5" pieces) for cauliflower & also very good.

entries.title: Slow Roasted Turkish Lamb
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Slow Roasted Turkish Lamb
entries.notes:
*Tender and delicious lamb made per recipe using both Maras pepper & pomegranate molasses shown as optional.  Fuyu are a fav for us but out of season so omitted from garnish, otherwise as specified.

*Made day ahead to Step 4 then separated meat from juices to cover & refrigerate both so fat could be easily lifted off.  Day of service, rough sliced lamb, reheated in defatted juices (didn't need reducing). 

*Easy prep, needs little tending, aroma "ahh..." some, on 'keeper' list.

entries.title: Strozzapreti With Roasted Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Strozzapreti With Roasted Tomatoes
entries.notes: Roasted the tomatoes and finished them with parsley / cheese mixture per recipe.  Very good.  Did not make the from-scratch pasta.  And while we have dried strozzapreti among the pasta "library" in our pantry it isn't one of our fav shapes.  So we used dried spaghetti noodles cooked al dente, drained and tossed tomatoes in with them, marrying with a little pasta water too.  Tasty.  The tomato mixture would likely also be very good on crostini or bruschetta.

entries.title: Juicy Blt
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Juicy Blt
entries.notes: Brilliant idea to marinate the tomatoes a bit in the vinegar and olive oil.  Color me embarrassed to not have thought of that before in decades of BLT making.  This is excellent.  (Without weighing in on the debate taking place here on order of assembly of ingredients between the uber crisp (definite good thing) toast because my hubby puts his together in a different order than do I.)  But we also found half the ingredients were plenty to fill two sandwiches.

entries.title: Classic Carrot Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Classic Carrot Salad
entries.notes: As my pantry is my witness, I keep juice-packed pineapple tidbits stored there just so I can make this kind of carrot salad.  (Well...on occasion when making pizza I add them to peppers and onions on "a pie".  But I will deny that in public.)  I choose brown raisins here and yogurt over mayo.  Seems plenty sweet to me without optional honey but your taste may vary.  I prefer lemon zest to orange.  And you'd be amazed what a difference fresh Farmer's Market carrots make grated for this.

entries.title: Crostini Alla Norma
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Crostini Alla Norma
entries.notes:
*Delicious outcome.  Made per recipe but for omitting 1/4 C fresh basil in step 4.  (We prefer basil in alla Norma dishes added at end and not simmered in with mixture.)

*Fresh tomatoes best but Petite Dice fire roasted (Muir Glen - Petite cut is key) @ 15 oz can drained works in pinch.

*Ricotta salata best but Grana Padano or Parmesan work too.

*Many eggplant dishes benefit from making ahead (ex: caponata) and this does too.

*If you want agrodolce (sweet/sour) flavors look for caponata.

entries.title: Spatchcocked Chicken With Herb Butter
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spatchcocked Chicken With Herb Butter
entries.notes:
This recipe calls for a 'modified' or partially spatchcocked chicken given the backbone is cut only on one side and left on the bird.  A fully spatchcocked chicken is even better to use.  You can see Melissa Clark's great 'how to' video at the following link. I always do it this "full" way - easy to do and to cook - and save backbones in freezer for stock.
 https://www.nytimes.com/video/dining/100000001076482/spatchcocking-a-chicken.html

entries.title: Herby Feta And Yogurt Dip With Sumac
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Herby Feta And Yogurt Dip With Sumac
entries.notes:
*Made as written.  Delicious flavors, good texture. 

*Full fat Greek yogurt (like Fage 5%) and quality feta (used my fav brine packed sheep's milk French brand) - not the pre crumbled stuff - are key.  

*Love sumac but next time I'll try it just on top as it does 'color up' the dip when stirred in.  

*Leftovers good on toast, to top oven roasted potatoes or sweet potatoes...

entries.title: Summer Shrimp Scampi With Tomatoes And Corn
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Summer Shrimp Scampi With Tomatoes And Corn
entries.notes: Made this absent the shrimp and it was a really delicious summer veg dish.

entries.title: Seared Scallops With Jammy Cherry Tomatoes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Seared Scallops With Jammy Cherry Tomatoes
entries.notes: Made absent the scallops.  The jammy cherry tomatoes are a delicious partner with burrata or on bruschetta or as part of cheese / charcuterie / olives and crostini plate for a simple meal.

entries.title: Roasted Zucchini With Garlicky Bread Crumbs And Mozzarella
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Zucchini With Garlicky Bread Crumbs And Mozzarella
entries.notes: "You'll be dining on your own, sweetie."  My comment to my hubby as he prepared this dish for dinner was because I love all veg except zucchini which it is not too big a stretch to say I loathe.  But this preparation was great!  I even had a second serving.  He made it per the recipe but for omitting anchovies.  And for step 1 he salted the zucch maybe 45 min. ahead and patted dry before putting under broiler (set on high).  Tender veg, crispy crumb topping above melted cheese.  Keeper!

entries.title: Colcannon With Crispy Leeks
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Colcannon With Crispy Leeks
entries.notes: Nice sized bunch of broccoli rabe for greens component was great partner for garlic, olive oil and German Butterball potatoes (from local farmer as 'tis the season here) in this mash and just harvested onions were allium choice.  This recipe was originally created as a way to cook tasty meals from our pantries during the midst of pandemic lockdowns.  It creates a good dish now too when things are a tad more opened up.  Cooking keeps me sane.  Thanks to Melissa Clark for her recipes for all times

entries.title: Oven Braised Guinness Beef Stew With Horseradish Cream
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Oven Braised Guinness Beef Stew With Horseradish Cream
entries.notes:
Delicious outcome for this dish made largely per recipe but for the following:
*didn't have Guinness or other stout so used Belgian Ale
*since not using stout, omitted espresso powder
*used water in place of beef broth & about 1/2C less than specified

Added the veg about 45 minutes into cooking time so they wouldn't overcook.  At 2 hr 15 min total cook time meat and veg were just right done.

Sauce / gravy / broth is yummy - country bread served to sop up every drop a good idea.

entries.title: Neck Bones Pork Neck And Noodles
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Neck Bones Pork Neck And Noodles
entries.notes:
Noting Ryan U's comment re this turning into a "pot of grease", it is true pork neck releases a LOT of fat when simmered.  What works best for me in making neck stock is a ratio of about 7C water to 3lb "ish" bones.  After 3 "ish" hours remove the bones. Strain, cool & refrigerate stock to let the big fat cap come to top.  Lift it off, reheat and use stock for all kinds of things.  (Ex: I use @ 2 parts neck stock + 1 part water for Mexican stewed beans & fat from cap to saute sofrito.)

Our Farmers Market has 4 farmers who have pasture raised pork and it is processed by a small local FDA approved slaughterhouse.  Every part of a pig is available to purchase.  Including necks.  If you have access to pasture raised pork I think you'll find there is a substantial flavor difference for all of it, including necks in a dish like this, to the big factory farmed and production slaughter house animals.  I buy humanely husbanded animals for my conscience.  But it benefits taste buds too.

entries.title: Green Rice Arroz Verde
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Green Rice Arroz Verde
entries.notes:
Made this just as written - except for reducing 1/4C oil to 2T which works well - maybe as many as a dozen times since discovering the recipe here a couple of years ago and outcome has been just right every time.  In the "either / or" of stock or water I always choose water.  Enjoyed serving it with a pot of Mexican style stewed cranberry beans (see Milk Street recipe)  tonight but the rice is delicious with so many things.

Simply excellent.  Simple to make.  Excellent to eat.  Made it exactly per recipe.  Outcome just as expected.  Very easy.  Delicous.  Keeper!  We could easily have served 3 or maybe even 4 people for smaller portions.  But so good that two of us finished it off to be full to the brim served alongside garden fresh tomatoes and some of Bittman's McBitty bean patties served sans bun.

entries.title: One Pot Meatballs And Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: One Pot Meatballs And Sauce
entries.notes:
Made twice for tender and delicious meatballs.  Mix is so tender that some tweaking on 2nd pass helped make them easier to handle and even more yummy (imho) in outcome.  Notes:

*Used all pork (hubby can't eat beef)
*1 egg worked better than 2
*1/3C crumbs worked better than 1/4C
*let ricotta-crumb panade mix rest 15 min in step 1
*once meat mix is together return to fridge for 30-60 min for easiest handling to form meatballs - makes all the difference
*even better 2nd day
*freeze well in sauce

entries.title: Stuffed Shells
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Stuffed Shells
entries.notes:
Forgot to add to previous note:

*One whole egg works as well as two yolks in our experience.

*Shells can be made & stuffed then cooled & put on a baking sheet or other flat tray in freezer & when frozen stored in zip loc bag until ready to use them - then put in dish with sauce to bake per recipe.

*Made as written & also with blanched chopped chard in filling. 

*Sauce is simple & tasty - we increase by 50%.

*Spare par-cooked & unstuffed shells can be cooled & frozen to use -  we discovered they were easy to handle just barely thawed to fill then & bake.

entries.title: No Bake Lemon Custards With Strawberries
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: No Bake Lemon Custards With Strawberries
entries.notes: Neighbors who are really good cooks invited us to dinner for a splendid meal at every step.  They ended with this indescribably perfect dessert.  Made exactly per the recipe said the talented cook.  I was tempted to lick the dish.  So I offer up these comments not as the one doing the cooking but as one who had the delightful experience of eating this lemony, just sweet enough but not to much, just rich enough but not overly so delight.  I plan to lick the dish when I make it at home!

entries.title: Sourdough Stuffing With Kale And Dates
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sourdough Stuffing With Kale And Dates
entries.notes: I use chili de arbol frequently in cooking and have a bag of them in my pantry.  In my experience they are small dried chiles and cannot be sliced as this recipe specifies.  Does anyone know if there are fresh chili de arbol (bird's beak chili)?

entries.title: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic And Rosemary
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic And Rosemary
entries.notes: Have made various turkey roulades several times & whether this recipe or others, I SO agree with cooks who have posted here about the importance of NOT rolling the skin in with the roulade but making sure the skin is kept on the outside.  Otherwise you'll end up with flabby skin roasted in the final product.  One of the best 'how to' videos for roulade turkey breast for technique is by J. Kenji Lopez-Alt.  See steps 3 and 4.  Can apply technique here. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mEofA0SvS1k

entries.title: Spicy Red Pepper Cranberry Relish
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy Red Pepper Cranberry Relish
entries.notes: Very good - made it twice - and a little change of pace for the umpteen batches of cranberry sauce I made during cranberry season.  We prefer more tart cranberry sauce so only used about 10 Tbsp sugar not 1C.  Omitted cayenne.

entries.title: One Pan One Pot Thanksgiving Dinner
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: One Pan One Pot Thanksgiving Dinner
entries.notes: Brava to Melissa Clark for the culinary choreography work that went into creating this menu and testing out the recipes and timing for the meal.  If you  haven't seen the 11 minute-ish video that goes with this, I so recommend it.  Melissa Clark is always so fun to watch cook.  And her contributions through the Times also are rewarding to cook. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SNx5wYGnh0k&feature=emb_logo  I'll try this sometime this winter.  Minus bacon on the turkey I think.  But fun concept!

entries.title: Buttered Stuffing With Celery And Leeks
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Buttered Stuffing With Celery And Leeks
entries.notes:
*Made for Thanksgiving last year, 2019 and it was delicous.  In this crazy 2020 year I made it for two of us to enjoy as a pre-Thanksgiving meal (since that will be pasta this year...why not) and it was just as yummy and easy to cut in half.

*To cut it in half  - used 7 oz total of torn sourdough (weight after drying) and 1 lg. egg whisked in with 1 1/4 C stock.  Proportions worked, next time I might use 2 sm eggs.

*In previous note about cutting this in half, should also have said I used a well buttered 8" square baking dish and fill was just right in that.

Made recipe as written except I tore bread into 1" pieces not 1.5 - 2".  Knowing bread pieces would 'grow' as they absorbed the liquid it seemed a better at end "eatin' size"  Was a good choice for us.   This is a delicious dish for more than Thanksgiving table to go with turkey.  Lots of roasted meats or veg would partner with this.  Or as we used it in our dry run...all by itself along with cranberry sauce and escarole salad it was a "just right" meal.

entries.title: Herby Bread And Butter Stuffing For Two
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Herby Bread And Butter Stuffing For Two
entries.notes: A very good recipe for reliable and tasty outcome made as written.  Also a very good base recipe for jumping off point if you want to experiment with different elements added or substituted. This would be a good one for 'first timers' to making dressing / stuffing to rely on for proportions and then easily doubled if serving four.

entries.title: Baked Polenta With Ricotta And Parmesan
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Baked Polenta With Ricotta And Parmesan
entries.notes:
Likely impossible to screw up this tasty dish.  IMHO after making 5 times & once trying w/o trying to screw it up myself - from memory when to stir in ricotta & getting way wrong & once when relative misread instruction.  Still delicious outcomes.  As long as you soak the polenta first -see previous note as like others I find no need to drain it - this seems foolproof.  Also found it works fine with half as much ricotta or a little less parmesan.  Half recipe serves 4 for us.

Excellent dish turned out exactly as expected based on recipe & photos.  Paired with "sort of" version of Alison Roman's pork meatballs for easy to make ahead comfort meal to eat while watching 2020 election returns.

Notes:
*cut recipe in half and still served 4 for our purposes,

*deviation from instructions - soaked polenta in water we cooked it in & given we weren't going to drain first, added a little extra (3Tish?) water to soak.

*only took 10 min for last cook in step 3

entries.title: Pumpkin Soup With Ancho And Apple
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pumpkin Soup With Ancho And Apple
entries.notes:
*Keeper made per recipe! Next time will add another ancho.  Used bit more sugar than specified.
*Easy to scale up or down depending on pumpkin on hand.
*Noting some used stick blender; don't think you'll texture right that way.  Texture is key element of this soup. Use blender or food processor.
*Sytrian (aka Austrian) pumpkin seeds (typically US grown) are better in a dish like this than bulk mass market seeds (typically China origin).  See Nuts.com, Amazon or WF to source.  I like OR growers.

entries.title: Brandied Dried Fruit
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Brandied Dried Fruit
entries.notes: Responded tongue in cheek to DT who said this makes a TON and recommended cutting it in half.  He/She is right in that this DOES make a lot and because this is my first pass at it, I scaled it down to about 1/4 of the recipe.  Easy to do.  Now in lidded glass jar I'll keep it in the fridge, turn a couple of times a day per instructions, and open it to use for the associated lamb recipe on Christmas day with lots left for the associated cocktail.  Aaahh-some aroma already.

entries.title: Pistachio Pinwheels
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Pistachio Pinwheels
entries.notes:
Claire Saffitz is an excellent instructor and with that in mind I highly recommend investing the roughly 9 minutes needed to watch and listen to her instruction as she demos making these cookies. 
 Great technique tips.  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4nshlz1FL-E  For those who mention the almond extract flavor competed too much with the pistachio, note Claire suggests using vanilla or lemon zest in the dough as alternative for those who don't like almond extract.

entries.title: Brussels Sprouts With Pickled Shallots And Labneh
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Brussels Sprouts With Pickled Shallots And Labneh
entries.notes: Loved it made as recipe was written.  Wouldn't change a thing.  Two of us polished this off as dinner because it was so tasty.

entries.title: Cherry Rugelach With Cardamom Sugar
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Cherry Rugelach With Cardamom Sugar
entries.notes: For bakers who have never made rugelach before, might be helpful to see Melissa Clark demo this delicious recipe by watching this youtube video.  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=05XZfvGTufk

entries.title: Tuna Noodle Casserole
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Tuna Noodle Casserole
entries.notes:
*Cut in half, used a 1.5 qt. (@12"L x 2"H) gratin dish & perfect fit.
*As contrast to folks who say they'd add more tuna, we found proportions of tuna to noodles to veg to sauce just right.
*Omitted mushrooms (not a fan of 'rooms & tuna textures together).  Added 'some' small dice red bell pepper; tasty & festive too for Christmas season.
*Omitted garlic & capers.
*Used panko rubbed with a little EVOO and a little kosher salt instead of chips. 2/3C just right for half recipe.

entries.title: Braised Lamb With Squash And Brandied Fruit
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Braised Lamb With Squash And Brandied Fruit
entries.notes:
*Christmas dinner was "winner" per hubby eating this dish made per recipe for ingredients but with minor 'tweaks' of instruction.

*Braised lamb day in advance.  Separated lamb from sauce to refrigerate to let fat rise overnight.

*Lamb perfect tender, removed from bone to reheat in sauce before serving. 

*Used tomatoes as in "tip" option.

*Started brandied fruit about 2 weeks in advance of using for this dish. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1021676-brandied-dried-fruit

entries.title: Lentil Soup With Smoked Sausage And Apples
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lentil Soup With Smoked Sausage And Apples
entries.notes:
*Wonderful flavors, easy to make, a keeper for rotation. Delicious day made, even better the next.

*Made per recipe for ingredients but starting with 8 oz lentils and easy to scale proportionally.

*Only change in technique was to chop celery in 1/4" not 1/2" and add in with the apples in step 1 instead of with garlic and herbs in step 2 as I wanted more time for celery to cook / soften.  It was a good choice for us.

entries.title: Lentil Soup With Chipotles
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lentil Soup With Chipotles
entries.notes: Totally tasty, easy and quick to make, healthy dish that reheats well too.  Keeper.  Made per recipe for ingredients and technique but cut in half to accommodate the remaining brown lentils in my pantry.  Very easy to scale down for all ingredients and used 1 small canned chipotle which was just enough smoky heat zing and not too much.  Be certain to toast and grind cumin seeds per recipe, using pre ground will not deliver the same flavor.

entries.title: Halvah
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Halvah
entries.notes: Good quality tahini is essential.  Choose brands like Soom (Ethiopian sourced sesame seeds) or Al Wadi (Lebanese and pretty easy to find in well stocked grocers) or similar.  Some other brands, like Once Again as someone mentions in a post, can use South American sesame seeds and are more bitter.  (I have used that brand too for personal experience.)  Also avoid Joyva, the ubiquitous one in can, for bitterness. Good tahini should not be over roasted.  Should also be pourable at room temp.

entries.title: Crispy Stuffed Mushrooms With Harissa And Apricots
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Crispy Stuffed Mushrooms With Harissa And Apricots
entries.notes:
*These were excellent made per recipe.  Keeper!

*Noting some others say they had leftover filling, I found it just right amount to compactly fill mushrooms w/no extra.  For context, I used cremini and 8 oz was 12 mushrooms. I wonder if different takes on what "1/2 small onion" is might be at root of too much fill?

*Made through step 5 about 90 min in advance and held at room temperature before step 6.  Worked well.

entries.title: Spicy And Tingly Beef
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Spicy And Tingly Beef
entries.notes: All on the ingredients list are in my pantry and fridge but for the Pixian doubanjiang.  Reading the ingredients in it, and realizing it will be a little different using a substitution, does anyone have familiarity with it to know if gochujang or sambal olek would work as I have both of those.

entries.title: Lemony White Bean Soup With Turkey And Greens
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lemony White Bean Soup With Turkey And Greens
entries.notes:
*Truly tasty outcome of nutritious soup requiring very little time and some steps can be done in advance making this even easier to do.

*We're not vegetarians but play at eating like them 80% of the time so eliminated turkey and subbed veg broth for chicken and delicious outcome.

*Tuscan kale (aka Lacinato) as our sturdy greens choice.  Parsley, mint, chives for herbs. 

*Made several hours ahead through step 4.  Then reheated to finish through step 6.  Worked well.

entries.title: Brown Butter Lentil And Sweet Potato Salad
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Brown Butter Lentil And Sweet Potato Salad
entries.notes:
*Remarkably good flavors from remarkably easy to make dish and 'bonus points' it used all staples in my kitchen for easy 'school night' meal.

*Brown butter vinaigrette is genius.  Liking higher acid dressings added a splash more vinegar and drizzle more maple syrup for balance.  Otherwise, as written and the dressing is 'it' in this dish.

*We chose sweet potatoes (only needed 1T oil to cook) among 'or carrots or butternut' options.

*This fed 3 for us.  Dish is at its' best warm.

entries.title: Turkish Style Lamb Boreks
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Turkish Style Lamb Boreks
entries.notes: Excellent outcome making recipe as written.  Has been a while since I worked with phyllo but fun to for creating the 'cigar' shape.  Since phyllo is something we don't often use and since the lamb and cheese fillings were so delicious in this combo we're starting to experiment with using them on flatbreads, inside pita, as savory bun fillings and so on.

entries.title: Creamy Vegan Hot Chocolate
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Creamy Vegan Hot Chocolate
entries.notes: Have made this as written several times in the last year or so --- hot chocolate helps make sticking to home base as part of pandemic recommendations go down --- and this is a delightful libation.  We like the combo of creamy almond butter and rice milk for the "either  / or" alternatives and we do use the optional sugar.  Sometimes also a pinch of ground chili powder or cinnamon too.  We aren't vegan so make dairy based hot chocolate too.  But this is a good one as well.

entries.title: Hot Italian Sausage And Broccoli Rabe Frittata
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Hot Italian Sausage And Broccoli Rabe Frittata
entries.notes:
In previous 'high five' note for this remarkable dish I said it was my "take to a desert island" recipe.  That was in 'before days' and now it has been 'pandemic days favorite' too.  We haven't been out to eat since 3/16/20 and it is 2/02/21 as I post this and our cooking at home audience is now 2 people.  Made this many times in easy to scale down to half size - so 4 portions means 2 night's dinners for 2 - by using 8" sq baking dish to assemble ingredients.

Adding to my earlier comments, this is now my "take to a desert island" recipe.  Made more than half a dozen times, each time with excellent outcome.  (I do cook the rapini to start in step 1 per Viola Buitoni's technique: http://zesterdaily.com/cooking/dont-bitter-just-cook-rapini-better ) Great for B L or D served hot, warm or even room temp.  Travels well. Reheats well.  Can be 1 dish meal.  Plays well w/ sparkling wine which I'll take to that desert island too.  Perfect.

A new "go to" for one of my fav veg, rapini (broccoli rabe) to introduce others to it.  Made 3 times, 2 per recipe and 1 w/out mozzarella.  Prefer w/out.   Also recommend Viola Buitoni's method of cooking the rapini.  Perfect outcome every time to balance natural bitterness of the veg.  Cook rapini like this then proceed with the recipe.  Delicious.  http://zesterdaily.com/cooking/dont-bitter-just-cook-rapini-better/  Also found leftovers for this frittata travel and reheat well.

entries.title: Olive Oil Refried Beans
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Olive Oil Refried Beans
entries.notes: Samin leads in the article, in which this recipe is discussed, by saying it is her last column for the magazine.  I will miss reading her columns as I hear her voice as I read.  She has taught me much and I am an old...umm...well seasoned...passionate home cook.  I also do not 'get' Instant Pots.  Thanks for making me not the only person to not use one.  And her technique here for refried beans leads to luscious outcome.  I eat beans like this for breakfast at times.  Truth.

entries.title: Birria De Res
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Birria De Res
entries.notes: A great recipe for birria, I made it with lamb (using this time osso bucco cross cut shanks from local rancher) which is always my favorite protein for birria.  The grated fresh ginger in this was a new to me element in birria recipe and it was a good add. I like to make day ahead and refrigerate so I can remove some of the fat at top then reheat to use.  In addition to tortillas you may want a spoon at the table to get every drop of spicy broth from the bowl.

entries.title: Goat Cheese And Fig Quick Bread
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Goat Cheese And Fig Quick Bread
entries.notes:
Excellent!  Made as written except increased figs from 4 to 7 based on comments of others who said they might double figs next time.  That choice first time was right for our taste buds.  Used dried Mission Figs (dark skinned) - we always have them. Baked up exactly as expected and using 9" loaf pan took 34 min to perfect  then removed from pan to cool per recipe. 
 Some commented this was dry; I wonder if they overbaked?  Great w/tea or wine or a Manhattan cocktail before dinner...or as dinner.

entries.title: Caramelized Onion Galette
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Caramelized Onion Galette
entries.notes: Sue Li is adorable and she finds watching Niles and Frasier Crane relaxing.  Two more reasons to enjoy her 'company' on this NYT Cooking video demo for her delicious galette.  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RDi9sCR7Uxk  Note at the end the garnish touch she does with the melted cheese off the parchment.  Very nice.  From my experience the onions benefit from longer time to caramelize.  And letting rest for 30 min before cutting is best too.  So good with Alsatian Pinot Blanc or Belgian Ale.

entries.title: Roasted Tomato And White Bean Stew
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Tomato And White Bean Stew
entries.notes: Where beans share starring role with fresh cherry tomatoes, it makes sense to cook beans from scratch if you can.  It makes such a flavor difference, imho.  This recipe is fun with giant butter beans too, like Royal Corona or Judion.  Cooking a pot of beans then dividing and freezing them in portions equivalent to canned beans makes having them on hand for recipes easy.   And if you tuck some parmesan rinds in with the beans as they cook then really good in this dish.

entries.title: Lentil Tomato Soup
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Lentil Tomato Soup
entries.notes: We enjoyed this simple, comforting, nutritious soup made very close to as written and reheated to eat the day after it was cooked.  For tomatoes I used a 14 oz can of Mutti brand canned cherry tomatoes.  Substituted thyme for rosemary.  Lots of black pepper and grated parmesan to finish.  Keeper for us.

entries.title: Steamed Artichokes With Vinaigrette Dipping Sauce
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Steamed Artichokes With Vinaigrette Dipping Sauce
entries.notes: Made just as recipe was written and outcome just perfectly delicious.

entries.title: Butternut Squash Pecans And Currants
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Butternut Squash Pecans And Currants
entries.notes: Made as written except for subbing blood orange vinegar for Champagne / white wine vinegar.  The recipe was a hit for flavor and pretty presentation both.

entries.title: Game Hens With Sumac Pomegranate And Cardamom Rice
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Game Hens With Sumac Pomegranate And Cardamom Rice
entries.notes:
1) Made with ingredients as written. So good for rice and poultry.
2) Re "Place seasoned birds in a roasting pan...drizzle all over with pomegranate molasses. Turn birds breast-side down and add water to pan to a depth of ½ inch."  I placed poussin on rack just above water not in it. 
3) Re "...  baste birds with pan juices frequently, until well browned." took longer than recipe specified to brown.
4) The rice is a keeper for lots of dishes.

entries.title: Amaro Sour
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Amaro Sour
entries.notes: Made as written x 2 for 2 and we both enjoyed this to be very happy we tried it - festive looking too in the lowball glass - but not enough to add it to the 'keeper' cocktail file at home.  To put our opinion in context I'll share that  hubby and I both enjoy bitter flavors, are fans of gin, he is a fan of Amaro and related bitter liquors, me not so much, I am a fan of all sour style cocktails, him not as much, and we both love grapefruit.

entries.title: Kukul Mas Maluwa Sri Lankan Chicken Curry
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Kukul Mas Maluwa Sri Lankan Chicken Curry
entries.notes: Kay, you ask where one gets pandan leaves in US.  Like fresh curry leaves, also more difficult to source, you can find them in Indian foods markets if you have one near you.  Also found in SE Asian foods markets as they are used in Vietnamese and Thai cooking.  Also via Amazon in dry form.  See sources like Spruce Eats for description of how to refresh dry leaves to use in recipes like this.  Pandan also comes in powder (even Kroger's owned Fred Meyer has that) but powder can't be used in this.

entries.title: Gold Rush
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Gold Rush
entries.notes: Re 3:1 ratio of honey to water to make the honey syrup for this yummmm....y libation, Bill asks "Every recipe for honey syrup that I have seen calls for one to one ratio of honey to water...Why the difference in this recipe?"  I guess my answer is "Because this is a different recipe."  It is a good one.  Honey a little more prominent at 3:1 than 2:1, flavor just a tad different using a darker honey v lighter one of course.  Endless possibilities for honey and bourbon type.

entries.title: Brothy Cod With Peas And Mushrooms
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Brothy Cod With Peas And Mushrooms
entries.notes: Easy to make as recipe is written and delicious to eat.  Also really good with fregola substituted for couscous - fregola and seafood are a classic partnership - and halibut in place of cod.  Keeper.

entries.title: Roasted Sausages With Grapes And Onions
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Roasted Sausages With Grapes And Onions
entries.notes: This is great as written using Italian pork sausages and really good using merguez, a spicy lamb sausage.  We like fennel seed when using pork sausages and cumin and/or coriander as the crushed spice options when using merguez.  We have used red grapes and black ones.  Always a good sherry vinegar to finish.

entries.title: Sausage Egg And Kale Casserole
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Sausage Egg And Kale Casserole
entries.notes: We enjoy making this with merguez (spicy lamb sausage) and given the seasonings in merguez (more North African flavor profile) we find fontina not quite the right option for the meltable cheese here using merguez...although it is a great option with Italian sausage in this.  For the meltable nature and similar texture consider mozzarella.  Also a good feta crumbled over can replace the grated parmesan when using merguez.

entries.title: Mezzelune Pasta With Peas And Shiitake Mushrooms
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Mezzelune Pasta With Peas And Shiitake Mushrooms
entries.notes: Making pasta dough from scratch is above my functional set (a disabled cook with balance problems due to MS) I opted to use top quality spinach - ricotta filled fresh ravioli made by an Italian couple and sold at Farmer's Market as starting point.  Then continued recipe as written for ingredients and steps 5 - 7. I did add a little lemon zest to the pan with the veg / sauce since the ravioli didn't contain it.  Delicious, even with purchased ravioli replacing the mezzelune.

entries.title: Kimchi Jjigae With Ribs
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Kimchi Jjigae With Ribs
entries.notes: Gochugaru and kimchi are staples in our kitchen.  Not so for green plum syrup.  Not certain I wanted to source it give recipe takes only 1 TBSP (although we have a Korean market in town) I read a couple of food blogs by Korean cooks to see what might be substituted.  Both similar to the info in the one linked here:  https://kimchimari.com/green-plum-syrup-%EB%A7%A4%EC%8B%A4%EC%B2%AD-maesil-chung/

entries.title: Gungjung Tteokbokki Korean Royal Court Rice Cakes
entries.content: NYT Cooking recipe: Gungjung Tteokbokki Korean Royal Court Rice Cakes
entries.notes: We are fans of Korean food flavors in our house and have a local Korean market from which we can source rice cakes.  But  as much as we enjoy Korean dishes and as good as are the flavors in this dish, we've tried rice cakes a couple of times now, both cooked by us at home like this and as cooked by a local skilled chef, and we just can't appreciate the texture of them.  Different strokes.  You really can't make this dish absent rice cakes.  They are the key ingredient.
